# 5. Bifurcation Analysis {#ch-bifurcation background-color="#6880af"}

## How to analyse a brain system

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-overview.png){fig-align="center" width="96%"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%" .smaller}
**Time series**
$x(t)$ at fixed $(a, x_0)$
:::
::: {.column width="33%" .smaller}
**Phase plane**
flow geometry at fixed $a$
:::
::: {.column width="34%" .smaller}
**Bifurcation diagram**
regimes as $a$ varies
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Three complementary lenses on the same system. The **time series** is what you'd
actually record — but it only shows one parameter setting. The **phase plane** shows
the *geometry* of the flow at a fixed parameter: where trajectories go, what's stable.
The **bifurcation diagram** is the summary — it tracks how the equilibria and their
stability change as we turn one knob ($a$). Goal for this chapter: learn to read that
third panel. It's a *map of regimes*, and it tells you what behaviours a model can
produce before you run a single long simulation.
:::

## Linear Systems


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%" .r-fit-text}
$$
\dot x = a\,x \quad\Rightarrow\quad x(t) = x_0\,e^{a t}
$$

- $x(t)$: State variable
- $x_0$: Initial condition
- $a$: Parameter

Solution:
$$
x(t) = x_0\,e^{a t}
$$

Equilibrium:
$$
\dot{x}(t) = ax(t) = 0?
$$
$$
\quad\Rightarrow\quad x_{eq} = 0
$$

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.r-stack }

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-stability.png)

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-stability-perturbation.png){.fragment}


:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Start with the simplest possible system — 1D and linear. Everything about stability is
in the sign of $a$. The solution is an exponential: if $a<0$ any perturbation decays
back to zero, so the equilibrium is stable; if $a>0$ it grows, so it's unstable; $a=0$
is the knife-edge. The equilibrium is always $x=0$, but its *stability* flips as $a$
passes through zero. The second figure makes this concrete: nudge the system off the
equilibrium and, when it's stable, it returns. Neural masses work the same way — we
linearise around a fixed point, and the sign of the eigenvalues tells us whether
activity settles back or runs away.
:::

## 2D portraits

::: {.r-stack}

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-phase-portraits-animation.gif){.fragment}

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-phase-portraits-static.png){.fragment}

:::

::: {.notes}
Two ways to read the same phase portraits. First the animation: watch the flow
reorganise as the parameter sweeps — fixed points appear, move, change stability, and a
closed loop (a limit cycle) emerges. Then the static snapshots: the same regimes frozen
side by side, left-to-right from a single stable resting state, through the bifurcation,
to a sustained oscillation. Takeaway: small smooth parameter changes cause *qualitative*
changes in behaviour — that's a bifurcation.
:::

## Regime-change building blocks

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column .smaller width=20%}

- Top: equilibria as $a$ varies.
- Bottom: local flow $\dot x=f(x)$ at the dotted parameter slice.

:::

::: {.column width=80%}
![](img/normal_forms.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
There's only a small zoo of *local* bifurcations — the normal forms. Top panel:
equilibria and their stability (solid = stable, dashed = unstable) as the parameter
varies. Bottom: the 1D flow at the dotted slice. The classics are saddle-node (two fixed
points collide and vanish), transcritical (they swap stability), pitchfork (one splits
into three), and Hopf (next slide). You don't have to memorise them — just know that
almost every regime change in a neural model is one of these in disguise.
:::


## Hopf: birth of an oscillation

$$
\dot z = (a + i\omega)z - |z|^2z,
\qquad r_{\mathrm{cycle}} = \sqrt a
$$

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation-hopf-birth.gif){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

::: {.smaller}
fixed point loses stability $\Rightarrow$ stable oscillation appears
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the important one for brain dynamics, because it's how rhythms are born. As $a$
crosses zero, a stable fixed point loses stability and a stable limit cycle appears
around it, with radius growing like $\sqrt a$ — so the oscillation starts small and
grows. Mechanistically: the system stops settling to a constant rate and starts
oscillating (think alpha or gamma rhythms emerging). One-sentence version:
**a Hopf bifurcation is where a quiet equilibrium turns into a spontaneous oscillation.**
:::


## Dynamical Regimes

<!-- ![](img/g2d_bifurcation.png){fig-align="center" width="82%"} -->

![](img/figures/bifurcation/fig-bifurcation_timeseries.png)

::: {.notes}
Tie it back to the time series — the thing experimentalists actually see. Each parameter
regime produces a characteristically different trace: a flat/noisy fixed point, a damped
transient, or a sustained oscillation. The bifurcation diagram predicts *which* of these
you'll get, so instead of guessing parameters by trial and error you read them off the map.
:::

## Numerical continuation {.r-fit-text}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](img/continuation_pseudo_arclength_white.png){height=600px}
:::
::: {.column width="38%" .smaller}
$$
F(x, a)=0,
\qquad (x,a)=(x(s),a(s))
$$

- follow branches through folds
- detect folds and Hopf points
- switch to periodic orbits

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
How do we compute these diagrams without brute-force simulating millions of settings?
**Continuation.** We solve $F(x,a)=0$ and *follow* the solution branch as $a$ changes —
like walking along a ridge. Pseudo-arclength parametrises by arc length $s$ so we can
turn around "folds" where the curve bends back on itself (a plain parameter sweep would
just fail there). Along the way we automatically detect folds and Hopf points and can
switch onto the periodic-orbit branch. This is what tools like BifurcationKit.jl do.
:::


## TVB simulation infused with bifurcation analysis

![](img/g2d_network_bifurcation.gif){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

::: {.smaller}
TVBO + tvboptim simulation of Generic2dOscillator on the Desikan-Killiany network. Nodes are colored by their instantaneous $V(t)$ and projected onto axial (top) and sagittal views; the regime map places each node at its current $(I_{\mathrm{eff}}, V)$ on the bifurcation diagram.
:::

::: {.notes}
Now scale it up to a whole brain: a Generic-2D-Oscillator on the Desikan-Killiany
connectome. Each node is coloured by its instantaneous state and placed on the shared
bifurcation diagram — so you can literally watch which regime each region is in, and how
coupling pushes nodes across bifurcations. The point: bifurcation analysis isn't just a
single-node toy, it organises the dynamics of the full network model.
:::


## Bifurcation analysis: hands-on

- Notebook: [`notebooks/4_BifurcationAnalysis/BifurcationAnalysis.qmd`](notebooks/4_BifurcationAnalysis/BifurcationAnalysis.qmd)
- Sweep input $I$ in a TVB neural mass model
- Continue fixed points and periodic orbits
- Read the diagram as a **map of regimes**

```python
result = exp.run("bifurcationkit.jl")
```

::: {.notes}
What you'll do in the notebook: take a TVB neural-mass model, sweep the input current
$I$, and continue both the fixed points and the periodic orbits. The mindset to keep:
read the resulting diagram as a *map of regimes* — for any parameter value it tells you
what the model will do. The continuation itself runs through the Julia backend in one line.
:::
